mnemonics.title: Encoder vs Decoder
mnemonics.content: ENCODER: 2^n INPUTS → n OUTPUTS (COMPRESS/ENCODE). DECODER: n INPUTS → 2^n OUTPUTS (EXPAND/DECODE). REMEMBER: E for Encode (Fewer lines out), D for Decode (More lines out).

mnemonics.title: Priority Encoder Priority Order
mnemonics.content: HIGHER INPUT INDEX = HIGHER PRIORITY. In a priority encoder, input with highest numerical index (like I7 over I3) typically gets priority when multiple inputs are active.

mnemonics.title: Encoder Output Equations
mnemonics.content: FOR 4-TO-2: Y1 = I3 + I2 (UPPER HALF), Y0 = I3 + I1 (ODD POSITIONS). REMEMBER: Y1 gets the higher-order bits, Y0 gets alternating pattern.

acronyms.usage: Controls whether encoder is active or disabled (EN=1: active, EN=0: disabled)
10-line to 4-line encoder converts decimal digits 0-9 to 4-bit binary code

acronyms.expansion: Handles Multiple Active Inputs by Priority
acronyms.usage: Resolves conflict when more than one input is HIGH simultaneously
Additional output in priority encoders to indicate if any input is active

visualTips.visualization: Draw a box with 4 input lines on left (I3,I2,I1,I0) and 2 output lines on right (Y1,Y0). Label which inputs affect which outputs: Y1 gets I3,I2; Y0 gets I3,I1.Think of it like a elevator floor selector - pressing floor button 3 gives output 11, floor 0 gives 00.
In a 2-output encoder truth table, outputs form binary counting from 0 to highest input position. For 4-to-2: 00,01,10,11 correspond to I0,I1,I2,I3 activation respectively.
Draw priority encoder with additional V output (valid bit). V=1 when any input is active, V=0 when all inputs are 0. This helps distinguish valid from no-input conditions.

analogies.analogy: Think of an encoder like a hotel room number system. If 100 guests arrive at once, the manager (encoder) needs to give each a room. Instead of 100 separate channels, they assign each guest a 2-digit room code (like 00,01,10,11). Multiple guests can't share, just like one input line should be active at a time for basic encoders.

A priority encoder is like an emergency call system in an office. When multiple emergency buttons are pressed simultaneously, the system doesn't get confused - it responds to the most critical emergency first, then the next. The priority is pre-determined.

A computer keyboard has 104 keys but only uses a few wires to the processor. This is like a 104-to-binary encoder - instead of running 104 separate wires, each key press gets converted into a compact binary code (like 8 bits) that travels through just 8 data lines.

Encoder is like ZIP compression. You have many files (many input lines) and compress them into fewer files (fewer output bits) for efficient transmission. The decoder then uncompresses to get back the original.
